coredata.dc:title: Adsorption of lysozyme by alginate/graphene oxide composite beads with enhanced stability and mechanical property
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiwei
Ma, Jianwei
Chen, Shaojuan
Huang, Yudong
He, Jinmei
coredata.dc:description: The large-scale applications of lysozyme in the pharmaceutical industry and food industry require more efficient and cost-effective techniques for its separation/purification. In the present study, graphene oxide (GO) was encapsulated into environmentally benign sodium alginate (SA) to prepare a Ca2+ crosslinked alginate/graphene oxide composite gel beads (Ca-SA/GO) which were then used to adsorb lysozyme from aqueous solutions. Compared with pure Ca2+ crosslinked alginate gel beads (Ca-SA), the as-prepared Ca-SA/GO has a lower swelling degree, an improved gel stability in salt solutions, and a higher mechanical performance. This can be explained by the uniform distribution of GO sheets in the Ca-SA matrix and the existence of hydrogen bonding and high interfacial adhesion between GO filler and SA matrix demonstrated by SEM, FTIR, XRD, and TGA. Batch adsorption experiments found that the lysozyme adsorption capacity of Ca-SA/GO can reach 278.28 mg g−1 and it can be regenerated and reused at least 4 times. Moreover, in column adsorption, the Ca-SA/GO showed excellent dynamic adsorption property. With good stability, adsorption capacity, and regeneration ability, the Ca-SA/GO could be a promising adsorbent for lysozyme from aqueous solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alginate
Graphene oxide
Mechanical properties
Lysozyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Swelling degree, calcium content, and structural stability analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the preparation routes of Ca-SA/GO. (b) Schematic showing the ionic crosslinks through Ca2+. (c) Schematic of the interaction between SA and GO.
(a) FTIR spectra of SA, GO, and Ca-SA/GO with different GO concentrations; (b) TGA (b) and DTG (c) curves of Ca-SA and Ca-SA/GO0.4.
SEM images of of Ca-SA and Ca-SA/GO gel beads (magnifications: 1000×).
(a) Swelling degree (left) and calcium content (right) of Ca-SA/GO with different GO concentrations; (b) Stability of Ca-SA/GO with different GO concentrations.

(a) Schematic illustration for the uniaxial compression of a single bead; (b) Typical force versus displacement curve obtained from compression of single bead; (c) Max compressive force during testing of Ca-SA/GO with different GO concentrations; (d) Estimated Young's modulus of Ca-SA/GO with different GO concentrations.

(a) Adsorption capacity of Ca-SA/GO with different GO concentrations (pH is 6, lysozyme concentration is 1 mg mL−1); (b) Change in the adsorption capacity of Ca-SA/GO0.4 composite beads with increasing cycle numbers; (c) Schematic illustration of the dynamic adsorption process by beads column; (d) Adsorption capacity of lysozyme by the beads columns (column length is 3 cm).

Particle-size values (Diameter, d
90
/d
10 , and Feret ratio) for wet gel beads.

head.ce:title: Adsorption of lysozyme by alginate/graphene oxide composite beads with enhanced stability and mechanical property

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textiles and Clothing, Qingdao University,Qingdao, Shandong 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The large-scale applications of lysozyme in the pharmaceutical industry and food industry require more efficient and cost-effective techniques for its separation/purification. In the present study, graphene oxide (GO) was encapsulated into environmentally benign sodium alginate (SA) to prepare a Ca2+ crosslinked alginate/graphene oxide composite gel beads (Ca-SA/GO) which were then used to adsorb lysozyme from aqueous solutions. Compared with pure Ca2+ crosslinked alginate gel beads (Ca-SA), the as-prepared Ca-SA/GO has a lower swelling degree, an improved gel stability in salt solutions, and a higher mechanical performance. This can be explained by the uniform distribution of GO sheets in the Ca-SA matrix and the existence of hydrogen bonding and high interfacial adhesion between GO filler and SA matrix demonstrated by SEM, FTIR, XRD, and TGA. Batch adsorption experiments found that the lysozyme adsorption capacity of Ca-SA/GO can reach 278.28 mg g−1 and it can be regenerated and reused at least 4 times. Moreover, in column adsorption, the Ca-SA/GO showed excellent dynamic adsorption property. With good stability, adsorption capacity, and regeneration ability, the Ca-SA/GO could be a promising adsorbent for lysozyme from aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Novel nanomaterials such as graphene, graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots, and their hybrids have been the fascinating materials in the field of adsorption [1,2], nanosensor [3–5], electrocatalyst [6], and electrochemical analysis [7,8]. Graphene oxide (GO) is a chemically modified highly oxidized form of graphene, consisting of single atom thick layer of graphene sheets with the carboxylic acid, epoxide, and hydroxyl groups [9,10]. GO has become one of the fascinating materials in the field of adsorption and it has been currently used as a forceful adsorbent for the removal of heavy metals, dyes, and some toxic pollutants [1,11]. Recently, GO has gained attention as a new adsorbent for proteins due to its large specific surface areas and layered structures, and it has demonstrated a strong and selective binding capacity for lysozyme [12,13]. However, there are some limitations limited GO's practical application: (a) It is difficult to recycle GO due to its micro-sized structures; (b) GO is very expensive and its laborious production involves the use and waste of toxic materials and high energy [14,15]. Therefore, searching for suitable supporters to immobilize GO for preparing macroscopic GO composites is an important strategy for its further application.

Sodium alginate (SA) is a very important polysaccharide in nature, which is mainly composed of (1, 4)-linked β‑d‑mannuronate (M) and α‑l‑guluronate (G) residue [16,17]. SA can be one of the ideal polymeric matrices for GO due to a series of merits such as high stability in organic solvents, biodegradability, being easily obtainable, and environmental friendliness [18,19]. In addition, SA has a unique property of crosslinking in the presence of multivalent cations, which is rather complex with GG sequences in the polymer chain to form the “egg-box” junctions [20,21]. However, pure ionic SA gels inevitably “swell” in the highly concentrated alkali/salt solutions and display limited stability [22–24]. Furthermore, SA gels fragile collapse as the ion exchange between divalent ions in the alginate and monovalent ions in solutions could drastically degrade and rupture the gel [22,25]. These unsatisfactory properties have often limited its further industrial applications of alginate gel beads. Various methods, such as blending [26], using a high concentration of Ca2+ coagulation [25], and covalent crosslinking [21] have been reported to improve the mechanical performance and stability of alginate gel beads, but the effect is not satisfying. The blending with other polymers or crosslinking with a high concentration Ca2+ usually sacrifices the carboxylate functional groups which are the preferable reaction site. In addition, covalent crosslinking agents such as glutaraldehyde and epichlorohydrin are toxic, which will restrain the biomedical applications of alginate beads.

To the best of our knowledge, no study on preparing calcium alginate immobilized graphene oxide composite gel beads (Ca-SA/GO) used for the adsorption of proteins has been reported. Moreover, how to prepare Ca-SA/GO with high-strength, better stability, and reusability has rarely been investigated. Therefore, the aim of this study is to utilize the excellent formability of alginate to support GO while use GO to enhance the structural stability and mechanical properties of Ca-SA/GO. Furthermore, batch and column adsorption experiments of lysozyme were applied to evaluate the protein adsorption performance and reusability of Ca-SA/GO.

Sodium alginate (pharmaceutical grade, M/G = 6:4) was kindly donated by Qingdao Hyzlin Biology Development Co., Ltd. (China). The viscosity of alginate is 1315 mPa.s (2 wt%, 25 °C). Calcium chloride (CaCl2) was of analytical grade and purchased from Spectrum Chemical Co., USA. Lysozyme powder (purity: 99%, Mr. ~14,000) purified from chicken egg white was obtained from Amresco Co., USA. All other chemicals were purchased from Fisher Scientific (Fisher Scientific Co., USA). All water used in this study was deionized water. Graphene Oxide (diameter: 500 nm–5 μm; thickness: 0.8–1.2 nm; single layer ratio: ~99%; C/O ratio: ~2.4) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd.

Different concentration GO solutions (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 mg mL−1) were prepared by using ultrasonication for 10 min. 2 g SA was added to 100 mL GO solutions and stirred vigorously for 6 h and ultrasonication for 3 min to obtain uniform SA/GO solutions. Then, the SA/GO solutions were dropped through a peristaltic pump into 100 mL coagulation solutions containing CaCl2 (0.16 mol L−1) under continuous stirring for 4 h (Fig. 1 ). The diameter of the needle used in this study is 0.8 mm and the flow rate of alginate solution is 100 mL h−1. The series Ca-SA/GO0.1, Ca-SA/GO0.2, Ca-SA/GO0.4, and Ca-SA/GO0.6 represent the Ca-SA/GO prepared by GO solutions with a concentration of 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 mg mL−1, respectively.
The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected by a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Co., USA) at 4000–400 cm−1 range with 64 scans and a 4 cm−1 resolution.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) were conducted with a Shimadzu TGA-50 apparatus (Shimadzu Scientific Instruments Inc., USA) with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from room temperature to 650 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere (flow rate: 30 mL min−1).
Structure analysis was performed using a diffractometer (X'PERT PRO MPD) with Cu radiation generated at 40 kV and 120 mA. The samples were scanned from 5 to 60° at a scanning rate of 4°/min.
The diameter of alginate beads was measured with vernier calipers, and average diameter was obtained through the measurements of 20 beads out of approximately 200.

d
90
/d
10 is defined as the ratio of the particle diameter where d90 is represents the value where 90% of the population is smaller than this size and d10 represents the size where only 10% of the beads resides below.

Feret ratio (FR) was calculated by:
(1)
Feret ratio
FR
= d / D

where d is the minimum Feret diameter, D is maximum Feret diameter. A Feret diameter is defined as the distance between two parallel tangential lines of a sphere. FR is a measurement of the degree of circularity, and FR of 1 indicates a perfectly spherical microsphere.

The swelling degree (SD) was calculated by using the following equation:
(2)
SD =
W w
−
W d
W d
× 100 %

where W
w and W
d are the weights of the wet and dry beads, respectively.
The calcium content was determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (Optima 8300, Perkin-Elmer Co., USA).

In order to assess the long-term stability of Ca-SA/GO in phosphate buffer solution (PBS), same number beads were dipped into 20 mL of PBS (0.1 and 0.2 M), pH 6. The solutions containing beads were standing for 24 h and then macroscopic images were taken. The pH values of the solution were measured by using a pH meter (XL600, Fisher Scientific Co., USA).

The mechanical properties of single wet bead were determined by a texture analyzer (A. XTPlus, Stable Micro Systems Corporation, UK). The test was performed at a constant speed of 8 mm s−1 and was stopped when the samples were crushed to the same degree of deformation. For each sample, 10 beads were measured. The force-displacement data and the peak pressure force that was displayed in the software interface were collected. According to previous reports [27,28], the Young's modulus of beads were estimated by the following equation:
(3)
F =
4
R
1 / 2
3
E
1 −
v 2
H 2
3 / 2
where F is the compression force (N), R is the initial radius (m) of a bead, E is the Young's modulus (kPa), v is the Poisson ratio (0.5), H is the displacement (m).

Lysozyme (with an isoelectric point of 10.8) solutions with a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 and a pH value of 6 was prepared using PBS. To test the static adsorption performance, 50 mg of as-prepared CA-SA/GO was immersed into 15 mL lysozyme solutions (15 mL) for 24 h, subsequently the absorbance intensity change at 280 nm was detected using the UV − vis spectrophotometer, and then the adsorption amount was calculated by using the following equation:
(4)
q e
= V
C 0
−
C e
/ m
where q
e is the adsorption capacity (mg g−1), V is the protein solution volume (mL), C 0 (mg mL−1) is the initial concentration of the protein solution, C e (mg mL−1) is the equilibrium concentration of the protein solution, and m is the amount of adsorbent (g). The UV − vis spectra were recorded by a spectrophotometer (Evolution 600, Thermo Scientific Co., USA) scanning from 240 to 400 nm at room temperature.

To investigate the reversible adsorption performance of the as-prepared CA-SA/GO, the adsorbed beads were incubated in NaCl solution (0.5 M) for 40 min after each cycle to elute the adsorbed lysozyme and then rinsed with ultrapure water for five times to remove the NaCl. Subsequently, regenerated alginate beads were immersed into the lysozyme solution to be reused for another cycle.

The dynamic adsorption of lysozyme was investigated by using a bead column. About 200 wet gel beads were packed into a polypropylene syringe (5 mL), and 3 mL of lysozyme solution (1 mg mL−1) were carefully filtered through the column at a flow rate of 1 drop s−1 and controlled by a pump. The eluted solution was collected and then added into the same bead column for the second filtration process. The same procedure was repeated for a total of three times.
All statistical analyses were performed using a universal analysis of variance. The data was presented as mean value ± SD.

FTIR experiments were carried out to investigate the interactions between GO, Ca2+, and SA. Fig. 2a shows the FTIR spectra of SA, GO and, Ca-SA/GO. For SA, the strong peak at 1595 cm−1 and a somewhat weaker peak at 1408 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of the –COO−, respectively [16,29]. And these two peaks are the most useful characteristic peaks to investigate the ion crosslinking of SA. After crosslinked with Ca2+, the symmetric -COO- peak shifted from 1408 to 1416 cm−1, which can be attributed to a strong interaction between the -COO- and the Ca2+ ions, as a result of the formation of “egg-box” structures [17,30]. Moreover, for both Ca-SA and Ca-SA/GO, the peak of CH– asymmetric stretching vibration at 2927 cm−1 is obviously weak because the “egg-box” structures limit the CH– stretching and lessens the changes in the dipole moment [31,32]. When GO was added, the –OH stretching vibration peak (3451 cm−1) of SA shifts to smaller wavenumbers (3408 cm−1), which can be attributed to the possible formation of hydrogen bonds between SA and GO as schematically shown in Fig. 1.

XRD was also used to verify the interaction between SA and GO. The XRD patterns of GO, SA, and Ca-SA/GO0.4 beads are shown in Fig. 2b. It is noted that no GO peak is observed in the Ca-SA/GO beads, which is consistent well with previous results for graphene oxide/sodium alginate scaffolds reported by Wan and coworkers [33]. This indicates that GO was well exfoliated and evenly dispersed in SA matrix.

To investigate the thermal tolerance difference, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) curves of pure Ca-SA and Ca-SA/GO-0.4 are shown in Fig. 2c, d. As showed in Fig. 2c, because both Ca-SA and Ca-SA/GO are mainly composed of alginate, their TGA curves are similar and both of them were quite stable even at the temperature over 150 °C.

Usually, according to the peak temperature (Tp) of the DTG curve represented the temperature at which the maximum weight loss rate was reached, the alginate undergoes three-stage thermal degradation processes: (1) the loss of volatile products through dehydration (from room temperature to 180 °C); (2) the thermal degradation of the polymer (180–350 °C); (3) the carbonization process (above 400 °C). As showed in Fig. 2d, since the samples have been dried fully before testing, there is no any mass loss peak in the DTG curves in the first stage, which is corresponding well with the TGA curves. In the second stage, both the two peak temperatures (at which the maximum weight loss rate reached) of the DTG curves of the Ca-SA/GO-0.4 are higher than that of pure Ca-SA, revealing that GO enhances the thermal stability of SA and retards the pyrolysis of the composite beads. This may be attributed to that the GO layers trammeled the movement of the SA molecule chains, impeded the thermal decomposition process, and increased the needed energy of thermal decomposition of Ca-SA/GO. However, due to the low content of GO in the composite, the effect was not very obvious.

To further determine the quality of the GO dispersion, SEM was employed. The SEM images of the Ca-SA, Ca-SA/GO0.2, and Ca-SA/GO0.6 beads after being air dried are shown in Fig. 3 . After air dried to constant weight, the beads show similarly acceptable spherical shapes, they become significantly shrunken and collapsed in a non-uniform way due to the air-oven drying condition. The surface of Ca-SA showed some buckling, and some agglomeration can be faintly seen. Conversely, the surface of the Ca-SA/GO0.2 and Ca-SA/GO0.6 showed a more smooth surface but also have some wrinkles and pores. This suggested that the GO sheets are uniformly distributed in the Ca-SA matrix and the good compatibility between SA and GO, corresponding well with the XRD results.

The particle size and distribution of the gel beads were determined by analyzing the beads in the wet state. As summarized in Table 1 , the mean diameter of Ca-SA/GO decreased with the increase of GO concentration, which could be attributed to GO increased crosslinking density and chains enlargement through hydrogen interaction and interaction with Ca2+. Moreover, compared with Ca-SA, Ca-SA/GO has a higher d
90
/d
10 and a lower Feret ratio, indicating a wide particle-size distribution and a lower degree of circularity were obtained. This may be explained by the fact that the beads are deformed and have no sufficient relaxation upon impact against the surface of the coagulation bath, as Ca-SA/GO drops have the faster coagulation progress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling degree, calcium content, and structural stability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Since hydrogel materials will swell in water, the swelling degree (SD) is one of the most important parameters to evaluate the crosslinking and interaction of hybrid/composite gel materials. Furthermore, the calcium content within the beads was determined by ICP-OES as the calcium content reflects the extent of the Ca2+ crosslinking and the SD was depended on the Ca2+ crosslinking density. Generally, the alginate bead has a low calcium content will have a high swelling degree. However, as shown in Fig. 4a, with increasing GO concentrations, both the calcium content and the SD of Ca-SA/GO decreased gradually. Furthermore, the decreasing scale in calcium content is not as sharp as SD (Fig. S1). The reason for this kind of abnormal phenomenon can be due to the aligned GO nanosheets and hydrogen bonds could restrict the mobility of alginate chains. Also, the strong interfacial interaction between SA and GO could obstruct and weaken the diffusion of water molecules in the matrix. Since excess water absorption may expand and deform the gel beads as well as weaken the mechanical toughness, the decreased SD caused by the addition of GO is beneficial. In addition, previous studies reported that the swelling suppression of hydrogels may help maintain their initial shape and retain their mechanical properties [34,35]. According to this, Ca-SA/GO may have better mechanical property than pure Ca-SA (which will be discussed later in Section 3.4), since it was not easily destroyable by the swelling forces.

Since hydrogel materials will swell in water, the swelling degree (SD) is one of the most important parameters to evaluate the crosslinking and interaction of hybrid/composite gel materials. Furthermore, the calcium content within the beads was determined by ICP-OES as the calcium content reflects the extent of the Ca2+ crosslinking and the SD was depended on the Ca2+ crosslinking density. Generally, the alginate bead has a low calcium content will have a high swelling degree. However, as shown in Fig. 4a, with increasing GO concentrations, both the calcium content and the SD of Ca-SA/GO decreased gradually. Furthermore, the decreasing scale in calcium content is not as sharp as SD (Fig. S1). The reason for this kind of abnormal phenomenon can be due to the aligned GO nanosheets and hydrogen bonds could restrict the mobility of alginate chains. Also, the strong interfacial interaction between SA and GO could obstruct and weaken the diffusion of water molecules in the matrix. Since excess water absorption may expand and deform the gel beads as well as weaken the mechanical toughness, the decreased SD caused by the addition of GO is beneficial. In addition, previous studies reported that the swelling suppression of hydrogels may help maintain their initial shape and retain their mechanical properties [34,35]. According to this, Ca-SA/GO may have better mechanical property than pure Ca-SA (which will be discussed later in Section 3.4), since it was not easily destroyable by the swelling forces.

Since the alginate-based materials have limited stability in monovalent ion solutions and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with a pH value of 6 was mostly used for proteins adsorption [36,37], we further investigated the structural stability of gel beads in 0.1 M and 0.2 M PBS (pH 6). Fig. 4b shows the structural stability of all of the examined beads investigated by immersion in PBS at room temperature for 24 h. After the 24 hs' immersion, the pure Ca-SA was nearly dissolved in 0.2 M PBS and became over-swelling, larger, softer, and easily broke when handled in the 0.1 PBS. This can be attributed to ion exchange between the Na+ in the PBS and the Ca2+ in the alginate structure under strong salt condition. In comparison to Ca-SA, Ca-SA/GO was relatively more stable and retained its overall size. Moreover, solutions from Ca-SA/GO soaked in PBS did not have visible turbidity while the Ca-SA solution became turbid. These results indicated that the structural stability of Ca-SA/GO could be enhanced by the GO. This can be explained as mentioned above: (a) GO could be well dispersed in alginate matrix, (b) GO could crosslink the alginate chains through hydrogen interaction, and (c) the increase of GO concentration could result in an enlargement in crosslinking.

The appropriate mechanical performance of beads is very important for their adsorption applications since it determines the stability during processing and application handling. The mechanical properties of the beads were evaluated from compression tests performed on wet Ca-SA and Ca-SA/GO composite beads. As shown in Fig. 5 , a single bead was compressed to approximately 50% deformation and then the compressional axis return to the start state. During compression, the normal force was measured at a given deformation, from which stress-strain curves were obtained. Representative force-displacement curves are presented in Fig. 5b. The stress-strain curves for all beads demonstrated the same general shape regardless of the concentration of GO. The Ca-SA/GO beads seemed to be more elastic than that of Ca-SA beads, which can be indicated by the higher force imposed on the Ca-SA/GO composite beads at a specific displacement compared to that of the Ca-SA beads. Moreover, as the Ca-SA/GO beads became more brittle, they cracked at lower strains at higher filler concentrations (ie Ca-SA/GO0.4 and Ca-SA/GO0.6).

Fig. 5c shows max compression force values during compression test for the different Ca-SA/GO composite beads. As expected, the mechanical properties of the beads depended on the GO concentration. It was found that the max compressive force tended to increase with increasing GO concentration, and a maximum compressive force of 12.1 N was achieved at 2 mg mL−1 GO concentration. When a Ca-SA/GO composite bead is subjected to compression stress, the GO nanosheets restrict the movement of the alginate chains for conformational change, resulting in a larger force being required to deform the Ca-SA/GO composite bead. In addition, as aforementioned, more space within the Ca-SA/GO composite hydrogel was being occupied by the GO nanosheets instead of water molecules, causing the bead to become more rigid.

As showed in Fig. 5d, Young's modulus of beads also positively correlated with the GO concentration. It can be observed that Young's modulus increased with the addition of GO to the beads, pointing to a stiffening effect. This has also been reported for sodium alginate/graphene oxide composite paper [38] and alginate fibers with a high concentration of single-walled carbon nanotubes [39]. This can be attributed to the physical interaction between the alginate and GO sheets and a fine dispersion of the GO sheets within the SA matrix. Also, these results are consistent with the data obtained in FTIR, XRD, thermal analysis, and SEM observation.

With a carboxylic functional group per unit, SA is a negative polyelectrolyte making it a suitable agent to interact with positively charged groups of some compounds or materials. This mechanism has addressed a majority of conventional adsorptive applications by taking SA as adsorbents [40,41].

According to Henderson-Hasselbalch Eq. (5),the ionic state of the carboxyl group is determined by the pH of the solution [42].
(5)
pH =
pK a
+ log
A −
HA

where pK
a is the dissociation constant, [A −] is the -COO- state of carboxyl group on (1, 4)-linked β‑d‑mannuronate (M) or α‑l‑guluronate (G) monomer; [HA] is the -COOH state of carboxyl group on M or G monomer.

The dissociation constants of the carboxyl group on G and M monomer are 3.65 and 3.38, respectively [43]. Since the pH used in this study is 6 which is higher than the dissociation constant of the carboxyl group (pK
a = 3.38). Therefore, the carboxyl group in gel beads are in the -COO- state, which can provide active sites for the adsorption of positively charged species. In addition, the isoelectric point (PI) of lysozyme used in this study is 10.8. In other words, the lysozyme will be positively charged at pH (6) < PI (10.8). Thus, adsorption (ion-exchange) of the cationic form of lysozyme onto Ca-SA/GO beads is possible at the working pH of 6. Here, we used lysozyme as model proteins to investigate the adsorption properties of Ca-SA/GO for positive proteins.

The influence of GO concentration on adsorption is shown in Fig. 6a, and it can be seen that the Ca-SA/GO have higher adsorption capacities under higher GO concentrations. And the Ca-SA/GO had a comparably high adsorptive capacity for lysozyme as compared to those reported in previous studies [36,37]. This improved adsorption capacities can be attributed to the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups located at the edges of GO sheets are introduced into Ca-SA, which makes more attachment sites exposed to accommodate more lysozyme. As a result, abundant –COOH in both SA and GO has good affinity with lysozyme through electrostatic interaction. Too little GO may not be sufficient to form stable and high-strength composite gel beads with super adsorption capacity, and too much GO will induce an increment of cost. Therefore, Ca-SA/GO0.4 with a GO concentration of 0.4 mg mL−1 was used in the following study.

In addition to adsorption capacity, reusability is another significant property that determines the practical applications of adsorbents. Reusability studies of Ca-SA/GO0.4 in lysozyme adsorption were conducted and showed in Fig. 6b. The adsorption capacity of the Ca-SA/GO0.4 decreased slowly during the 4 successive adsorption/desorption cycles and the Ca-SA/GO can be used 4 times (or 3 cycles) with the adsorption capability of lysozyme was maintained more than 80%. Considering the pure Ca-SA can't be survived in PBS (Fig. 4b), the GO played an important role in improving the adsorption capacity and reusability of Ca-SA/GO.

A fixed Ca-SA/GO beads column at laboratory scale was set up and used to investigate the dynamic adsorption of lysozyme (Fig. 6c). Fig. 6d shows the adsorption capacity of lysozyme by the column. After eluting three times, the lysozyme concentration in the eluted solution was decreased from 1.00 to about 0.066 mg mL−1. Previous studies have shown that an increase in bead height will improve the adsorption capacity as the number of binding sites correlate with the amounts of sorbents in the column [44]. Considering the bed height used in this study is only 3 cm, this dynamic adsorption result is excellent. Furthermore, these results confirmed the Ca-SA/GO’ ability in adsorption lysozyme from water solutions in the practical applications.

GO was incorporated into SA to prepare composite gel beads. The Ca-SA/GO showed to have enhanced mechanical property and salt resistance, lower swelling ratio and better thermal stability than the pure Ca-SA. This could be attributed to the strong hydrogen bonding interactions between GO sheets and SA chains demonstrated by FTIR, XRD, and TGA. The batch adsorption capacities of Ca-SA/GO for lysozyme enhanced when GO was added, and the adsorption capacity can still keep considerable values after 4 adsorption/desorption cycles. Moreover, in column adsorption, the Ca-SA/GO showed excellent dynamic adsorption property. Therefore, recombination action between SA and GO could lead to significant improvement in mechanical, structural and thermal stability of Ca-SA and make Ca-SA/GO as a promising candidate in adsorption of lysozyme from aqueous solutions.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Decreasing scale of swelling degree and Ca2+ content at different GO concentrations.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.03.023.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (No. HC201707).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide/alginate beads as adsorbents: influence of the load and the drying method on their physicochemical-mechanical properties and adsorptive performance
